claims.claim: Nvidia lost $588.8B in market cap and fell ~17% on January 27, 2025 after DeepSeek R1's release — the largest single-day market-cap loss in stock-market history at the time.
Nasdaq fell 3.1% and S&P 500 fell 1.5% on Jan 27, 2025, while the Dow rose 0.7% — the DeepSeek shock was concentrated in tech/AI, not a broad-market crash.
Satya Nadella posted 'Jevons paradox strikes again! As AI gets more efficient and accessible, we will see its use skyrocket' on X the day after DeepSeek R1's release.
SemiAnalysis estimated DeepSeek had spent well over $500M on GPUs and roughly $1.6B on server capex, contradicting the popularized '$5.6M training cost' figure that fueled DeepSeek-shock coverage.

Nvidia did not recover to its pre-DeepSeek-shock high (~$153, Jan 7 2025) until roughly 5.5 months later, around mid-July 2025, coinciding with the reversal of the H20 China export ban rather than a clean, immediate 'Jevons rescue.'

The ARC Prize's own leaderboard shows the cost to reach a comparable ARC-AGI-1 score fell from $4,500/task (o3 High, 88%) to $11.64/task (GPT-5.2 Pro X-High, 90.5%), a ~390x improvement — the primary source of the widely-recirculated 'cost collapse' statistic, which is specific to one benchmark, not general AI inference cost.
A 2026 arxiv survey argues the 390x ARC-AGI efficiency figure reflects reduced test-time sampling/parallelism engineering rather than a genuine algorithmic efficiency leap.

Jevons Paradox requires price-elastic demand for the resource in question; standard economic explainers contrast elastic goods (where the paradox can occur) against inelastic goods (where it cannot), and applying it to AI inference is not automatic.

Anthropic's own internal analysis found that each 1% increase in API price is associated with only a 0.29% reduction in usage — a real but 'meaningful, not dominant' elasticity, not the extreme elasticity a strong Jevons story would require.

Moonshot AI released Kimi K3 on July 16, 2026: a 2.8-trillion-parameter model with a 1-million-token context window, priced at $3/$15 per million input/output tokens; full open weights were targeted for July 27, 2026, and as of launch the model was proprietary API-access only, not yet open-sourced.

On Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index, Kimi K3 ranked #4 of 189 tested configurations (Index score 57.1), comparable to Claude Opus 4.8, behind Claude Fable 5 (59.9) and GPT-5.6 Sol (58.9); on Arena's general Text leaderboard its ranking was materially lower (roughly #6-9) and marked preliminary.
Marvell fell 8.71% and Intel fell 5.84% on July 16, 2026 (closing data), while AMD fell 5.33% the same day.

Nvidia fell 2.40% on July 16, 2026 and a further 1.04% on July 17, 2026 (cumulative ~3.4% two-day decline) — a real decline, but far smaller than the 'Nvidia barely moved' framing that circulated based on intraday snapshots.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) fell 4.29% on July 16, 2026 and was essentially flat (-0.01%) on July 17, 2026 — a combined ~4.3% two-session decline, not the ~10% figure that circulated in some recirculated commentary.

The widely-cited '$1 trillion in chipmaker value lost' and 'PHLX -10% over two sessions' figures originate from an unrelated Broadcom-earnings-driven selloff on June 5, 2026, six weeks before Kimi K3, and were being recirculated as if describing the July 16-17 event.

Widely-cited claims that Alibaba rose ~5-9% amid the Kimi K3 selloff are misattributed: Alibaba's real multi-day surge (roughly +11-17%) occurred around July 8, 2026, driven by a DOJ settlement, a Pentagon litigation stay, and Nvidia H200 China cloud-access approval — a distinct catalyst more than a week before Kimi K3. Alibaba was flat-to-down (-0.17% to -1.66%) on the actual July 16-17 selloff dates.

Nvidia's fiscal-2026 guidance has, for multiple consecutive quarters through mid-2026, assumed $0 of China data-center compute revenue — China exposure is already de-risked out of Wall Street's forward numbers, unlike January 2025 when China revenue was still a live unknown.

Nvidia's forward P/E fell to roughly 23.2x by July 2026, down from roughly 40x a year earlier (July 2025) — even as its market cap roughly doubled, meaning valuation multiples compressed as earnings growth outpaced price appreciation.

Nvidia crossed a $4 trillion market cap for the first time in July 2025, a full year before the Kimi K3 shock — by mid-July 2026 it traded near $5.1-5.15T before falling to roughly $4.86T intraday during the selloff.

S&P Global downgraded Oracle from BBB to BBB- (one notch above speculative grade) on July 9, 2026, citing AI-capex leverage and OpenAI revenue concentration risk — one week before the Kimi K3 shock, evidencing pre-existing financing-structure concern.
Michael Burry disclosed a short position against Nvidia and warned publicly about aggressive GPU-depreciation-schedule accounting risk on July 13, 2026, three days before the Kimi K3 release.

Market estimates put the scale of circular AI financing arrangements (Nvidia-OpenAI-Oracle-Microsoft-AMD-CoreWeave and related deals) at over $800B, with OpenAI's own disclosed infrastructure commitments totaling roughly $1.15T across seven vendors (Broadcom $350B, Oracle $300B, Microsoft $250B, Nvidia $100B, AMD $90B, AWS $38B, CoreWeave $22B) — a pre-existing concern well before Kimi K3.

Goldman Sachs' prime-brokerage data showed hedge funds net-sold semiconductor/hardware single-name stocks for four consecutive weeks through July 3, 2026, while continuing to buy broad index/ETF products — direct positioning evidence of rotation within the AI trade predating the Kimi K3 print.

In the week ending July 10, 2026 — days before Kimi K3 — semiconductor ETFs were among the top inflow recipients in the entire US ETF market (SOXX alone pulled in $5.3B), showing investors were still buying the chip-sector dip immediately ahead of the shock.

OpenRouter data shows Chinese-origin models exceeded 30% of US-company token usage every week since February 8, 2026, peaking near 46%, versus an 11% trailing-12-month average and just 4.5% in H1 2025 — a large, real usage-share shift.

OpenRouter's platform-wide token throughput scaled from roughly 5 trillion tokens/week in April 2025 to over 20 trillion tokens/week in April 2026 — the aggregate 'pie' roughly quadrupled even as its composition shifted toward Chinese open-weight models, partially supporting a Jevons-consistent reading at the platform level.

The actual OpenRouter-listed providers serving popular Chinese open-weight models (Kimi, DeepSeek, GLM) — GMI Cloud, Together AI, Fireworks AI, DeepInfra, Parasail — run predominantly on Nvidia H100/H200 GPU cloud infrastructure, not Huawei Ascend or alternative silicon, meaning rising Chinese-model usage share does not straightforwardly translate into lost Nvidia demand.

An AI startup (Lindy) moved 100% of its production traffic from Anthropic's Claude to DeepSeek citing multi-million-dollar savings — a documented case of direct vendor substitution rather than incremental new demand.

Kimi K2 (July 2025) and Kimi K2.5 (January 2026) produced no comparable US chip-stock selloff — analysts explicitly predicted K2 would trigger only a 'much slower style DeepSeek Moment' given it lacked R1's virality drivers, and Nvidia crossed its first $4T market cap in the same week as K2's release rather than selling off.

Z.ai's GLM-5.2 open-source release in mid-2026 triggered a large positive rally in Zhipu's own Hong Kong-listed shares (+32.8% close, intraday +48%) rather than a US semiconductor selloff — the inverse reaction pattern of a US chip-stock shock.

TSMC reported Q2 2026 revenue growth of 33.7% YoY (USD) on July 16, 2026 — the same calendar day as the Kimi K3 announcement — and raised FY2026 capex guidance to $60-64B; CFO Wendell Huang explicitly declined to give a specific multi-year forward capex figure when pressed by an analyst.

TSMC's 'CoWoS packaging sold out through 2026' language originates from CEO C.C. Wei's June 4, 2026 shareholder meeting remarks — six weeks before the Kimi K3 shock — describing a pre-existing capacity backlog, not a post-shock demand confirmation.

None of the big-four US hyperscalers (Alphabet, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) had reported Q2 2026 earnings as of July 17-18, 2026 — their scheduled report dates (Alphabet July 22; Microsoft/Meta July 29; Amazon July 30) all fall after the Kimi K3 shock, meaning the ~$725B combined 2026 capex guidance cited as evidence of intact AI demand is entirely pre-shock (set at Q1 2026 earnings in late April).

Combined FY2026 capex guidance across Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon and Meta stood at roughly $725B (up ~77% from ~$410B in FY2025), all set at Q1 2026 earnings in late April — the baseline against which any post-Kimi-K3 revision in the July 22-30 reporting window should be judged.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated that compute required for agentic AI workloads has increased roughly 1,000% compared to earlier generative-AI-era compute needs, attributing this to agents reading, reasoning, and generating far more tokens per task than simple chat completions.

A March 2026 Gartner analysis found agentic AI tasks require 5-30x more tokens than a standard chatbot interaction, with complex multi-agent systems consuming 200,000 to 1,000,000+ tokens per task and agentic coding workflows averaging 1-3.5 million tokens per task including retries.

OpenRouter's own State of AI data shows programming's share of token usage rose from roughly 11% at the start of 2025 to over 50% by year-end 2025, with agentic/agent-driven workflows generating more than half of all output tokens on the platform.

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said the company had planned for 10x usage/revenue growth in Q1 2026 but actual annualized growth was roughly 80x, attributing the compute shortage directly to this surge, driven substantially by coding/agentic use.

H100 GPU cloud hourly rental rates fell roughly 64-75% from $8-10/hr in Q4 2024 to $2-3/hr by early 2026, as more than 300 new GPU cloud entrants added supply — a real-time, sentiment-independent demand proxy that, unlike stock prices, points to oversupply of older-generation compute even as next-generation Blackwell/B200 capacity remained reservation-only and scarce.

Epoch AI's supply-chain analysis found the top four AI chip designers consumed roughly 90% of global CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity and HBM memory supply in 2025 while accounting for only about 12% of advanced logic-die (3nm-class) production — evidence that packaging and memory, not raw chip fabrication, were the binding 2025 supply constraint, independent of any AI-usage elasticity question.

SK Hynix and Micron reported their entire 2026 HBM production already sold out, per Counterpoint Research data — a second, independent supply bottleneck (alongside grid power) that limits how much incremental AI demand can convert into shippable chip revenue regardless of Chinese-model competitive dynamics.

The Federal Reserve held its policy rate at 3.50-3.75% at its June 17, 2026 meeting (the rate prevailing during the Kimi K3 shock) — 75 basis points lower than the 4.25-4.50% rate in effect during the January 2025 DeepSeek shock — indicating a nominally looser monetary backdrop this time, even though the June 2026 dot plot had turned more hawkish.

The VIX spiked 45% intraday to 21.56 on January 27, 2025 (a panic-grade move), versus rising only to roughly 18.0 (about +13%) on July 17, 2026 — a materially calmer volatility reaction to the Kimi K3 shock than to the original DeepSeek shock.

Nvidia's next earnings report (Q2 FY2027) is scheduled for August 26, 2026, with pre-shock guidance of $91.0B revenue (±2%) — the first hard, forward-looking data point on whether AI chip demand held up after Kimi K3.

No fresh public 'Jevons Paradox' restatement from Satya Nadella, nor any Kimi K3-specific statement from Jensen Huang, was found in the July 16-18, 2026 window, despite financial media explicitly drawing the same DeepSeek-moment analogy that originally produced Nadella's January 2025 framing.
